locked_facts: Critical angle: sin θ_c = n₂/n₁, valid only when n₁ > n₂ (denser to rarer)
TIR occurs ONLY when light travels from denser to rarer medium AND θᵢ > θ_c
At θᵢ = θ_c, the refracted ray grazes along the interface (θ_r = 90°)
At θᵢ > θ_c, Snell's law gives sin θ_r > 1 which is impossible — so no refraction occurs
TIR CANNOT occur when light goes from rarer to denser medium — there is no critical angle
Applications: optical fibre (light trapped by repeated TIR), diamond sparkle (θ_c ≈ 24.4° due to high n = 2.42), mirages
truth_statements: Total internal reflection occurs when light travelling in a denser medium strikes the interface with a rarer medium at an angle greater than the critical angle
The critical angle is the angle of incidence in the denser medium for which the angle of refraction in the rarer medium is exactly 90 degrees
Total internal reflection cannot occur when light travels from a rarer medium to a denser medium
Optical fibres work on the principle of repeated total internal reflection — light is trapped inside the core because the core has a higher refractive index than the cladding
The critical angle for diamond-air interface is about 24.4 degrees, which is why diamonds sparkle — light undergoes TIR at most internal surfaces

aha_moment.what_to_show: Increase the angle of incidence gradually from below the critical angle. At θ_c, the refracted ray runs exactly along the surface (θ_r = 90°). Go just 1° beyond θ_c — the refracted ray VANISHES completely and ALL light bounces back into the denser medium. This is not a gradual process. It is a sharp phase transition: below θ_c there is always a refracted ray, at θ_c it grazes the surface, above θ_c it disappears entirely. The math explains why: sin θ_r = (n₁/n₂) sin θᵢ would exceed 1, which is physically impossible.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three simultaneous rays: one below θ_c (partial refraction + weak reflection), one at exactly θ_c (refracted ray along surface), one above θ_c (100% reflection, zero refraction). The dramatic disappearance of the refracted ray at θ_c is the aha moment.

simulation_states.label: Below critical angle — partial refraction and reflection
simulation_states.description: Ray in denser medium (glass, n = 1.5) hits the glass-air interface. Both a refracted ray (in air) and a reflected ray (back in glass) are shown. The refracted ray bends away from the normal because it enters the rarer medium.
simulation_states.emphasis: Below the critical angle, light partially refracts into the rarer medium and partially reflects. Both rays coexist. The reflected ray is weaker than the refracted ray.

simulation_states.label: At critical angle — refracted ray grazes the surface
simulation_states.description: Angle of incidence equals the critical angle (θ_c ≈ 41.8° for glass-air). The refracted ray runs exactly along the interface surface, making θ_r = 90° with the normal. The reflected ray is now as strong as the incident ray.
simulation_states.emphasis: At the critical angle, the refracted ray just barely escapes, running parallel to the interface. sin θ_c = n₂/n₁ = 1.0/1.5 = 0.667, so θ_c = 41.8°.

simulation_states.label: Above critical angle — TOTAL internal reflection
simulation_states.description: Angle of incidence exceeds θ_c. No refracted ray exists — ALL light reflects back into the denser medium. The reflection follows the law of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). A TIR label is prominently displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Beyond θ_c, Snell's law requires sin θ_r > 1, which is impossible. So refraction cannot occur — 100% of the light reflects. This is total internal reflection.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — find the critical angle
simulation_states.description: Sliders to change the angle of incidence and the refractive indices. Watch the transition from partial refraction to TIR. Try diamond (n = 2.42) to see θ_c ≈ 24.4°. Try water (n = 1.33) to see θ_c ≈ 48.8°.
simulation_states.emphasis: Find the exact critical angle for different materials. Notice: higher n₁ means smaller θ_c, which means TIR happens more easily. This is why diamonds sparkle so much — their very small critical angle means most internal rays undergo TIR.

common_misconceptions.belief: Total internal reflection can happen in any direction — from rarer to denser also
trigger_phrases: TIR can happen air to glass also
common_misconceptions.correction: TIR can ONLY happen when light travels from a denser medium (higher n) to a rarer medium (lower n). When going from rarer to denser, the ray bends toward the normal and there is no critical angle — refraction always occurs at every angle of incidence.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two scenarios side by side: glass-to-air (TIR possible above θ_c) and air-to-glass (refraction always occurs, no TIR at any angle). The asymmetry makes the rule clear.

common_misconceptions.belief: TIR is just very strong reflection — some light still passes through
trigger_phrases: thoda light pass hota hai TIR mein
TIR mein bhi refraction hota hai
common_misconceptions.correction: Total internal reflection means 100% of the light is reflected — ZERO light is transmitted into the second medium. This is not strong partial reflection; it is a complete, sharp transition. Below θ_c, some light refracts. At and above θ_c, absolutely none refracts. There is no gradual transition — it is a phase boundary.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a reflection percentage meter: below θ_c it reads 10-50% reflection. At θ_c it jumps to 100%. Above θ_c it stays at 100%. The sharp jump from partial to total is the key visual.

legend.meaning: Total Internal Reflection — 100% reflection, zero refraction
Refractive index of denser medium (where the ray starts)
Refractive index of rarer medium (where the ray would refract into)
